questions.question_text: A salesperson creates a social media ad for a listing that displays only the salesperson's name and photo. What is missing?
questions.correct_answer: The brokerage name and the agent's license number
distractors: The MLS number of the listing
The property's legal description
The broker's personal phone number
questions.explanation: Under NRS 645.315(1) and NAC 645.610, every advertisement must disclose the name of the brokerage with which the licensee is associated, and the licensee must include his or her license number in a conspicuous way. These rules apply to all media, including social media ads. MLS numbers, legal descriptions, and the broker's phone number are not required in advertising.

questions.question_text: An agent designs a yard sign where their personal name appears in large bold letters and the broker's name appears in small text below. Does this comply with Nevada law?
questions.correct_answer: No, the brokerage name must be clearly prominent
distractors: Yes, if the broker name is visible
Yes, yard signs are exempt from this rule
No, only the broker name may appear on signs
questions.explanation: NAC 645.610(1)(c) requires the brokerage firm's name to be clearly identified with prominence in every advertisement. The Division evaluates the style, size, and color of the font and the location of the brokerage name. A sign that buries the brokerage name in small text beneath large personal branding risks violating this standard, and signs are advertisements under NAC 645.610(4).

questions.question_text: A team within a brokerage advertises under the team name 'Desert Dream Homes' without any broker identification. What violation occurred?
questions.correct_answer: The brokerage name must also appear in the ad
distractors: Team names are prohibited in Nevada
Only the team leader's name is required
Team advertising requires Division approval
questions.explanation: Nevada permits team advertising, but every ad must still disclose the brokerage name (NRS 645.315(1), NAC 645.610(1)(c)). Under NAC 645.611, a team name must also contain the name and license number of at least one member, the team must include more than one licensee, and all members must work for the same broker. No separate Division approval is required.

questions.question_text: An agent sends a mass marketing email about available properties that contains no broker identification. This type of ad is classified as what?
questions.correct_answer: A blind ad, which is prohibited
distractors: A permissible personal communication
An exempt electronic solicitation
A compliant informal marketing piece
questions.explanation: An ad that fails to identify the brokerage is commonly called a blind ad and is prohibited in Nevada. NRS 645.315(2) bars a salesperson from advertising solely under his or her own name, and NAC 645.613 deems unsolicited email and Internet marketing to be advertising subject to all advertising rules, including the brokerage-name and license-number requirements. Email is not exempt.

questions.question_text: Which statement about Nevada real estate advertising is correct?
questions.correct_answer: All ads must be truthful and not misleading
distractors: Print ads have stricter rules than digital ads
Websites are exempt from broker name rules
Agents may omit broker name on personal blogs
questions.explanation: Nevada requires all advertising to be truthful and not misleading, and the same rules apply equally to electronic and print media, websites, social media, and email. No medium is exempt from the requirement to include the broker's licensed name or from the prohibition on misleading content.

questions.question_text: A salesperson is asked by a lender to prepare, for a fee, a written opinion of a property's probable selling price. May the salesperson prepare this broker price opinion?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, if the license is active and in good standing
distractors: No, only brokers or broker-salespersons may
No, only licensed appraisers may prepare one
Yes, but no fee may be charged for it
questions.explanation: Under NRS 645.2515, any person licensed under chapter 645 — broker, broker-salesperson, or salesperson — may prepare a broker price opinion and charge a fee if the license is active and in good standing and the BPO contains the required contents. A salesperson's BPO must name the broker with whom the licensee is associated, and a BPO prepared for a lienholder may not be used in place of an appraisal to decide whether to approve a mortgage loan.

questions.question_text: A broker issues a BPO and presents it to the client without any disclaimers. What requirement was overlooked?
questions.correct_answer: A disclaimer stating it is not an appraisal
distractors: A copy must be filed with the Division
The client must sign an acknowledgment form
A second broker must verify the opinion
questions.explanation: Nevada requires that every BPO include a disclaimer stating it is not an appraisal. A BPO is a written opinion of probable selling price, which is different from a formal appraisal. The disclaimer protects consumers from confusing the two and is required under NRS 645.2515.

questions.question_text: What is the primary distinction between a broker price opinion and an appraisal in Nevada?
questions.correct_answer: A BPO is not a formal appraisal
distractors: A BPO uses comparable sales but appraisals do not
An appraisal may be issued by a broker
There is no legal distinction between them
questions.explanation: A broker price opinion is a written analysis or opinion of the estimated price of a property that any chapter 645 licensee may prepare under NRS 645.2515. It is explicitly not an appraisal, which requires a licensed or certified appraiser under NRS 645C and follows different standards. Both may use comparable sales data, but they serve different legal purposes.

questions.question_text: A broker prepares a BPO for a lender but fails to include any assumptions or limiting conditions. What requirement was missed?
questions.correct_answer: BPOs must state assumptions and limitations
distractors: Only appraisals require stated assumptions
Limiting conditions are optional for BPOs
The lender is responsible for adding conditions
questions.explanation: Nevada law requires BPOs to include a statement of intended purpose, property description, basis for the opinion with market data, any assumptions or limiting conditions, date of issuance, disclosure of interests, and licensee signatures. Omitting assumptions or limiting conditions fails to meet these requirements.

questions.question_text: A lender relies on a BPO to make a lending decision and later claims the BPO was misleading because it lacked a disclaimer. What risk does the broker face?
distractors: No risk because BPOs are informal opinions
Liability only if the BPO was intentionally wrong
The lender assumed the risk by using a BPO
questions.explanation: Failing to include the required disclaimer that a BPO is not an appraisal violates NRS 645.2515 and exposes the broker to disciplinary action. BPOs are not informal opinions but regulated documents. The broker has an obligation to comply with all BPO requirements regardless of the lender's reliance decisions.
